FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Contact: 
JOHN KEPLINGER 
916/322-5662 
'V ' 
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE 
Wednesday, Jan. 11 
LEGISLATORS MAY ATTEND LOBBYISTS' LUNCHEON 
The Fair Political Practices Commission ruled today that members 
of a lobbyist organization may entertain legislators at weekly luncheons 
provided 
is no behind-the-scenes arrangement between lobbyists to 
wine and dine lawmakers. 
The Commission made it clear, however, that if any evidence 
develops to indicate the existence of an 
icit or implicit agreement 
or understanding among the lobbyists to violate the law's $10 per month 
spending limitation it will crack down on the luncheons. 
California's Political Reform Act, which the Commission admin-
isters, prohibits 
ists from spending more than SIO a month to 
entertain individual legislators or other state officials. 
The Commiss 
'5 decision was issued in an opinion requested 
by Merle J. Goddard, president of the Institute of Governmental 
Advocates, an organi ation of approximately 50 lobbyists which holds 
weekly luncheons 
Legislature is in session. 
The organization asked the Commission if members could invite 
legislators as 
sts to the luncheons and 
for their food and 
age if the a 
e of s 
off ials oc 
on a random and 
infrequent 
sis. 
(more) 


hegislators 
Attend Lobbyists' Luncheon--2 
The Commission approved 
but only if the lobbyist members acted 
independently and did not arrange among themselves which legislators 
would be invited. 
"Although the instant situation presents a close case, we 
conclude that the random and infrequent attendance by officials and 
the lack of member's knowledge of another member's guest demonstrate 
the lack of an implicit agreement or understanding among IGA members 
to make gifts to an of cial totaling more than $10 in a month," 
Commission's opinion said. 
# 
1-78

sections.conclusion: Although the instant situation presents a close case, we conclude that the random and infrequent attendance by officials and the lack of member's knowledge of another member's guest demonstrate the lack of an implicit agreement or understanding among IGA members to make gifts to an official totaling more than $10 in a month.
sections.question_synthetic: May members of a lobbyist organization entertain legislators at weekly luncheons and pay for their meals, and if so, under what conditions does this comply with the $10 per month spending limitation on gifts to state officials?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC approved lobbyist members entertaining legislators at weekly luncheons provided that attendance is random and infrequent, members act independently without arrangement regarding which legislators are invited, and there is no implicit agreement among lobbyists to circumvent the $10 monthly spending limit. The Commission warned it will take action if evidence later emerges of any illicit or implicit agreement to violate this limitation.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Press release format rather than traditional advice letter; no formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections but contains clear legal analysis and ruling. Document dated January 11, 1978. Some text appears damaged/OCR errors (e.g., 'hegislators' instead of 'Legislators'). Contact person and phone number included for media inquiries.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May members of a lobbyist organization entertain legislators at weekly luncheons and pay for their meals, and if so, under what conditions does this comply with the $10 per month spending limitation on gifts to state officials?

CONCLUSION: Although the instant situation presents a close case, we conclude that the random and infrequent attendance by officials and the lack of member's knowledge of another member's guest demonstrate the lack of an implicit agreement or understanding among IGA members to make gifts to an official totaling more than $10 in a month.

The Commission made it clear, however, that if any evidence develops to indicate the existence of an icit or implicit agreement or understanding among the lobbyists to violate the law's $10 per month spending limitation it will crack down on the luncheons. embedding.summary: The FPPC issued an opinion approving the Institute of Governmental Advocates' practice of allowing member lobbyists to entertain legislators at weekly luncheons and pay for meals, provided attendance is random and members do not coordinate invitations. The Commission emphasized strict enforcement if evidence emerges of any coordinated arrangement to exceed the $10 per month gift limitation.
